summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers' lack of assertiveness in negotiations with executives and managers leads to unrealistic project commitments that ultimately harm the business. Drawing on Steve McConnell's insights, he explains that technical staff often cave too easily when pushed on estimates because executives naturally probe for discomfort points. The post clarifies that assertiveness isn't about being aggressive, but about communicating openly as an equal participant. Atwood recommends Dale Carnegie's classic book and practical steps like public speaking or improv classes to build these skills.
key_points: Technical staff lose negotiations with executives not because their estimates are wrong, but because they aren't assertive enough to defend them
Executives assume technical people will push back when necessary, but they typically don't, sending implicit signals they can deliver more than they actually can
True assertiveness is about being straightforward and honest while seeking win-win outcomes, not about being aggressive or confrontational
The business ultimately loses when developers don't fight for realistic estimates, leading to months or years of pretending something impossible is achievable
Assertiveness is a learnable skill that can be developed through deliberate practice like public speaking or improv classes
money_quotes: As software developers, we're great at communicating with computers. But we're typically not so great at communicating with other people.
The business loses, because it sets up a situation in which it pretends for months or years that it can do something that it can't.
They assume, incorrectly, that technical staff will be assertive with them if they need to be, and that isn't the case.
Technical people who don't push back hard enough, soon enough, are implicitly sending a message that they can do more than they really can.
There's a reason that they're executives!
To be assertive is not, as some people imagine, to be overbearing and aggressive, but to be straightforward, open and honest.
Show a genuine interest in the people you meet, and you'll find it reciprocated tenfold.
key_insight: When developers fail to assertively defend their estimates, they inadvertently deceive the business into believing impossible commitments are achievable.
